name: Sicilian Defense - Najdorf
description: The most popular response to 1.e4. The Najdorf variation with a6 is one of the sharpest.

moves.commentary: The Sicilian - an asymmetrical response that fights for the center.

children.commentary: Nf3 develops the knight and prepares d4.
d6 supports c5 and prepares Nf6.
d4 opens the center. The Open Sicilian.
Black takes the pawn. White will recapture with the knight.
Nxd4 recaptures and develops. The knight is well placed in the center.
The Najdorf. a6 prepares b5 and prevents Bb5.

defenses.name: English Attack
defenses.profile: The English Attack targets the kingside.

strategy.keyIdea: Create queenside counterplay with ...b5 and ...Bb7 while White attacks on the kingside — an intentional imbalance where both sides race.
strategy.middleGamePlan: Push ...b5-b4 to undermine White's center and expand on the queenside. Meanwhile, be prepared for White's kingside pawn storm with f4-f5. The Najdorf is not passive — you need to generate active counterplay or White's attack will overwhelm you.
strategy.watchOut: Reacting to White's kingside threats defensively — the Najdorf is a counterattacking opening and passive defense usually loses.
typicalGoals: Develop the bishop to b7 for long-range queenside pressure
Castle queenside to keep your king safe from kingside storms
Counter White's f4-f5 push with ...e5 or ...d5 when timed correctly

quiz.question: White has just played f4-f5 in a Najdorf. You haven't castled yet. What's your priority?

options.text: Castle kingside immediately to get your king safe
options.explanation: Castling kingside walks into White's pawn storm — in the Najdorf, queenside castling is usually correct when White attacks on the kingside.
Correct. The Najdorf demands counterplay. Pushing ...b4 attacks White's queenside while White attacks your kingside — whoever gets there first wins.

options.text: Bring the knight back to defend f6
options.explanation: Retreating pieces to defend passively plays into White's hands. The Najdorf is a counterattacking opening — you must create threats, not just block them.

default.opponentMoveSummary: Your opponent stepped off the main theory line with an unusual move, choosing their own path rather than the expected response.
default.whyBookBreaks: The standard line assumes a particular pawn or piece configuration that this move bypasses.
default.opponentStrategy: Your opponent is trying to reach a less-charted position where their preparation or intuition gives them an edge.
forwardGuidance: Prioritize completing your development — get all minor pieces off the back rank.
Control the center with pawns or piece pressure before launching any flank operations.

c3.opponentMoveSummary: White played c3, controlling d4 without allowing the typical ...d5 break (Alapin Variation after 1.e4 c5 2.c3).
c3.whyBookBreaks: The Najdorf assumes White plays 2.Nf3 and 3.d4. c3 avoids the Open Sicilian and the ...d5 central break.
c3.opponentStrategy: Alapin Variation — White chooses a solid, positional approach that controls d4 and avoids sharp Najdorf theory.
forwardGuidance: Develop normally; the Alapin often leads to slower, strategic play.
Consider ...d5 when appropriate to challenge White's center.

Nc3.opponentMoveSummary: White played Nc3, developing the knight and preparing a rapid f4-f5 kingside attack.
Nc3.whyBookBreaks: The Najdorf main line assumes 2.Nf3 and 3.d4. Nc3 leads to Grand Prix–style systems with early kingside aggression.
Nc3.opponentStrategy: Grand Prix Attack setup — White develops quickly and aims for f4-f5 before Black completes development.
forwardGuidance: Complete your development; White's kingside attack can be dangerous if you're not ready.
Challenge the center with ...d5 or ...e5 when the time is right.

f4.opponentMoveSummary: White played f4, launching immediate kingside aggression and giving up central control for initiative.
f4.whyBookBreaks: The main line assumes 2.Nf3 and 3.d4. f4 (McDonnell Attack) skips central control in favor of attack.
f4.opponentStrategy: McDonnell Attack — White plays for immediate kingside aggression and initiative, accepting a weaker center.
forwardGuidance: Strike in the center with ...d5 or ...e5; White's f4 weakens their position.
Develop your pieces actively; White's aggression can backfire if you respond correctly.
